story.title: Dr. Rodriguez and Your Body's Army
story.text:
Hello, I am Dr. Elena Rodriguez. I am an immunologist. My job is to study your body's army. This army is your immune system. It fights an invisible war every day.

Imagine a dangerous invader enters your body. This invader is a pathogen, like a virus. Your body must fight back. Special soldiers called white blood cells find the invader. They send signals to the whole system. The system then makes special weapons. These weapons are called antibodies. Each antibody is made for one specific pathogen. They lock onto the invader and stop it.

This first fight can be slow. Your body gets sick while it learns to attack. This is where my work is important. I help design a vaccine. A vaccine is like a training mission for your army. It shows your immune system a weak or dead piece of the pathogen. Your body learns to make the right antibodies without getting sick. Now, your army has a memory. If the real invader comes, your body attacks very fast. The strong response keeps your infection from getting severe.
think_abouts: What is the 'army' inside your body, and what does it fight?
How is a vaccine like a training mission for your immune system?
story.go_deeper: The flu virus changes its 'disguise' every year. How does this explain why we need a new flu vaccine each year?

margin_defs.definition: Something harmful that enters from the outside.
The ability to remember a pathogen and fight it faster next time.

check_prompts: What is the name of the Y-shaped protein that targets a specific pathogen?

vocabulary.definition: A germ, like a bacterium or virus, that can make you sick.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The flu is caused by a pathogen that enters your body.

vocabulary.definition: A special protein that your body makes to fight a specific pathogen.
vocabulary.use_sentence: My body produced an antibody to fight the cold virus.

vocabulary.definition: A shot that teaches your body how to fight a pathogen before you get sick.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The doctor gave me a vaccine to protect me from the measles.

vocabulary.definition: A special cell in your blood that acts like a soldier to fight infections.
vocabulary.use_sentence: A white blood cell attacks germs to keep you healthy.

discussion_cards.prompt: If a vaccine is a 'training mission' for your immune system, what happens during a real infection if you are not vaccinated?
discussion_cards.frame: If a person is not vaccinated, their immune system might be slow to respond because ___.

discussion_cards.prompt: How does having more white blood cells and antibodies help you feel less sick when a pathogen attacks?
discussion_cards.frame: More white blood cells and antibodies help by ___ which means the infection severity is ___.

discussion_cards.prompt: Dr. Rodriguez designs vaccines. Why is this job important for keeping a whole community healthy?
discussion_cards.frame: A vaccine researcher's job is important for the community because ___.

reflection_frames.knew: Before reading, I already knew that ___
reflection_frames.learned: From the story, I learned that ___
